what_teacher_said: Your 16th notes were really even – I was really impressed with your control and your ability to move throughout the broken chords while remaining consistent with tempo and sound.

I’d like to hear a little more flexibility in your wrist and more expression – try to add more dynamics, more crescendos, stronger forte passages and softer piano sections, and loosen up your wrist with up‑down and side‑to‑side motion.
If your teacher has told you not to be more expressive, you’re free to ignore my suggestion – it’s your artistic choice.
Wow, you play so beautifully and expressively – I loved the drama, the dynamic range, your phrasing and shaping. It was a lovely interpretation of the piece.

Make sure the left‑hand (or non‑melodic) notes stay really quiet, below the dynamic level of the melody. Practice the left hand alone, first in rhythms until it’s comfortable, then work on a lighter tone so it sounds like a different instrument.
Work on getting a more consistent voicing and shaping in the right hand – keep the phrasing clear and the drama steady throughout the piece.
Let me show you what I mean – (demonstrates a slower, lighter left‑hand motion with a relaxed wrist, like a little balloon or petting a cat).
Keep your wrist free and focus on the follow‑through from the opening of the hand to the note, then pull back and recover.
Use your torso, back and wrist, and play with the pads of your fingers close to the keys; staying close is a smart approach for pyrotechnical pieces.
Distribute the weight throughout the whole hand; avoid any wasted motion so nothing gets distorted.
Pay close attention to pedaling – it’s never about the pianist, it’s always about the sound and the music.
There is no room for flexibility with syncopation because it doesn’t fall on the strong beat.
It’s still pianissimo; why are you playing louder just because you like it? That’s not good enough – let’s erase it and start again.

You chose to play louder, but the E‑flat is already present earlier, so the louder dynamic doesn’t add anything special. If you want to highlight the tension, playing softer will actually make the effect stronger.
Playing it louder destroys the purpose of the phrase; a softer dynamic will give a much stronger result for what you’re trying to express.
The change you need comes from stopping the pedal – the pedal has been held, then you create a moment of silence, release half, and then play softer.
After that passage you must finish with a very clear, definite cadence that says ‘enough of this flirting with diminished ninths.’
I’d like a little bit more colors and more rubato – study how Friedman does rubato and try to work on that.
Just play the background, not the melody yet – you’re improvising a hopeful/peaceful mood that isn’t fully expressed.
That slight lift‑up should stay in the pedal, but overall it shouldn’t be expressive yet – don’t take too much time, you haven’t begun the piece.
The idea of rubato is important here – you need to use it, but don’t avoid it.
You can’t just say ‘more rubato’; you have to have a reason for it – think about where you’re going and take time.
I need more pedal here – if you make a statement, let the pedal support it.
Try not to get too soft – keep the dynamic level where it was perfect before.
You’re missing the E‑flat – it’s a very important note for the long line.
The middle voice of the left hand is too loud – balance it down.
When the chord dies down, lift the pedal and make a decrescendo at that point.
Make little phrases on the E‑flat – don’t let it become one long line.
The middle section should differ in sound and feeling from the earlier turbulent part – think of a hopeful, romantic chorale with a single soprano voice.
Add more singing quality and a little more turbulence – avoid too much diminuendo.
It needs more colors and to open up a little after you played it so beautifully.
No pedal here – playing it as a distant Bach would be better; the passage is too pretty with pedal.
Try to sound less impressionistic and more active – use less pedal so the texture doesn’t get lost.
Work on those three places that need to grow – keep developing until you reach the climax.
I have to hear the D‑flat bass note – if it’s too loud it spoils the rest of the texture.
It feels too relaxed – this theme should be very passionate, but don’t go too fast.
Much more time on the third phrase – now it’s much clearer to understand.
Start off really slowly, work out a fingering and stick with that. Write it in your score and keep it.
Play with a very articulate, slightly raised finger – each finger is played with a separate articulation.
Practice passages with a finger staccato; each note should be very clearly articulated.
Do not use extra arm movements that you won’t use in the final product; avoid arm‑staccato, use only the tip of the finger.
Practice with accents – start by accenting every other note to group in twos, then accent every fourth note on the main beats.
Work through different rhythmic patterns, such as dotted rhythms, and pay attention to the release of effort on the long note.
When holding a long note, think of it as a release of effort rather than ‘relaxing’ – stay completely free and let the hand reset instantly for the next note.
Group three notes together and connect the quick notes to the following long note; feel the motion toward the next long note.
Practice stopping on notes that give you trouble – for example, stop just before a ‘fluffy’ C‑natural, then finish the group, and later stop exactly on the problematic note.
Make a chain: start with a short group of notes, add another beat, keep extending forward (or backward) until you reach the top of the passage.
Exaggerate the musical shape by adding hairpin crescendos and diminuendos; make the line vocal and expressive so that when you speed up a residue of that shape remains.
Use a basic lateral wrist movement – swing the wrist left to right with a very free motion – to help the arm stay relaxed during runs.
Incorporate forearm rotation – a rocking, rotary movement that comes from the forearm – rather than relying only on finger pistons.
Add small circular arm movements to avoid a clattery, mechanical sound; combine them with fingertip flutter when you play faster.
Keep your fingers energized, never passive – combine rotary and circular arm movements so the whole arm works together with active fingers.
You only play at one volume, the maximum, which eliminates the ability to show emotion; you need to vary the dynamics.
Try pulling back and pushing the volume to create a more varied texture instead of staying loud all the time.
To improve improvisation, start by mastering a few basic ideas—like left‑hand octaves or a simple rhythm—then expand your catalog of techniques.
Playing everything with chord shapes, octaves, and at full volume makes every song sound the same and emotionless; you need contrast.
When you tone it down to a single chord while shouting the lyrics, the phrasing between voice and piano doesn’t match and confuses the listener.
Consider whether the chord you play suggests a timid, introverted feeling while your vocal delivery sounds aggressive—what emotion are you really conveying?
When playing a Dorian scale, make sure the tonal centre (C in this case) feels like ‘home’; finish on a chord that reinforces that centre.
An octave symbol means you should play the same note eight steps higher; we learn everything within one octave and then transpose as needed.
We don't play the piano just with our fingers – you need whole‑body choreography. Sit correctly with your feet firmly on the ground and balance on a firm stool so your sitting bones can move freely.
Watch the ‘gliding arm’: the elbow should lead the hand in a smooth line. My bad example shows a thumb that lacks mobility – that’s why the arm glides poorly.
When playing a scale, keep the thumb under the hand and avoid dropping the elbow onto the thumb. Notice how my elbow stays in one place while the thumb moves under the hand.
Use a Hannon exercise re‑fingered 1‑2‑1‑2 (or 1‑3‑1‑3) and make full use of the length of the key, sliding in and out rather than using jerky positions.

Switch from a thumb‑under to a thumb‑over attitude for the crescendo at the top of the G‑minor scale – lift the thumb with a rotary forearm movement. This gives strength and is completely tension‑free.
The wrist and forearm must stay free and flexible. Keep the elbow still while all adjustments happen in the wrist and forearm; moving the elbow produces the common arpeggio mistake.

Apply the wrist‑forearm exercise to a real piece: hold the thumb down, play the surrounding notes with the exact fingering you’ll use in the final arpeggio, keep the movement loose and rotary, then add extra notes as you become comfortable.

I would love to see a little more flexibility and looseness in your arms so you can play with different colors and touches; your articulation is nice but could be a bit more graceful with a loose arm and wrist.
My artistic ear wants those notes held a little longer and played more legato so the melody flows; avoid cutting the note values so short that they sound like rests.
Notice how I keep a really loose wrist and use tiny half‑pedal motions to carry the notes without becoming overly legato or dry.
Even if the score says forte, consider adding a little more drama here and play louder to let the multiple voices shine together.
When playing very quietly, make sure your arm weight goes all the way down to the key bed; this helps produce a clear, resonant tone even at low dynamic levels.
Your trills and ornaments are getting lost; practice them with the rhythm technique and flash‑practice technique to make them sparkle.
Avoid over‑using your fingers; keep them rounded and glued to the keys so you have flexibility, speed, and precision.
Keep your fingers on the edge of the keys; when they drift toward the wood you get stuck and lose control. Watch my incorrect vs. correct hand position.
Your wrists are a little bent, creating tension; try to maintain a 90‑degree angle by adjusting keyboard height so you can use the whole arm weight.
Make sure your fingers stay nice and rounded on the keys, just as we discussed with Christina.
First of all, Christina, that was gorgeous—I love your playing and the solid technique you and your teacher have built.
Alfredo, you sound absolutely fabulous; considering you’ve only been studying on your own for about a year, you’re doing amazing work.
Most of you play well, but it's a dynamic‑free zone and that's not good.
You have to add dynamics – just because the score says forte you must really play forte, not pianissimo all the time.
Follow the crescendo markings on the page; do the dynamics that are written in the score.
When you play forte, give the note a little more bite on the attack.
If the soloist makes a mistake, it’s your job to keep going and cover it – don’t stop, just keep the music flowing.
When the other player comes in late, don’t stop playing; you can tell them afterwards, but keep the momentum.
Be careful not to over‑balance her part – you still need to play forte when it’s marked.
Don’t stop when she rests; stay ready and jump back in as soon as she plays again.
Avoid playing loudly in the strings’ range; keep the piano part lighter there and reserve the forte for the marked passages.
On stage you need a larger persona – act confident like a first‑chair player and lead the ensemble.
Play with a clearer tone and listen to the overall texture; let the piano have its own colour.
Make your part more detached – start with a strong attack, then match her entry.
Add more bite and tighten the articulation on this passage.
When you’re in the strings’ range, don’t play too low; keep the sound drier and shorter to avoid covering them.

All of these exercises have to be done with attention to quality of sound and quality of movement – tension has no place in piano playing. We must really be listening and thinking; mindless repetition is absolutely out.
I lift my second finger up high, bring it down curved, feel the stretch. I’m showing a little movement to stay physically loose the moment I reach the key bed, even though there’s a slight grip.
Notice my thumb dangling off the edge of the keyboard – I use it to stretch the thumb, but don’t over‑stretch; that can be injurious.
Hold the third finger at rest on the bottom of the key and play the other fingers around it. Try it at home – it’s not as easy as it looks – and you can even play them staccato.
We don’t play the piano only with our fingers; we also use wrist rotation, circular arm movements and forearm rotation to drive the pattern.
Use a metronome: start a scale in quarter notes, then move to eighths, triplet eighths, then sixteenth notes while keeping the same pulse. This is a very good way of developing speed in scales.
When practicing double notes, enable a little lateral freedom of the wrist – don’t lock all the fingers; allow the wrist to move side‑to‑side.
This extended arpeggio exercise loosens the arm and connects the hand to the shoulder; it feels like a massage and gives a wonderful sense of relaxation.
If I were a beginner piano student again I would focus extra hard on learning and understanding chord structure – chords are everything.
Listen to these two short chord progressions and tell me which sounds more satisfying; the second sounds better because it ends on the I‑chord (C‑E‑G).
Practice with the A‑method (Absolute Accuracy Rule): you may ignore rhythm for a moment, but you must play the correct notes 100 % before moving on.
Play a small section repeatedly until you can execute it correctly 5‑7 times in a row before you move to the next part.
If a student can’t keep a steady beat, gently tap their shoulder on each beat to give them a physical sense of pulse.
Do a little bit of sight‑reading every day, starting with very easy pieces, and build up gradually.
Avoid relying too much on tools like Synthesia; they focus on individual notes and can limit your progress once you reach a certain stage.
Set high goals for yourself but also learn to have fun and relax at the piano; balance challenge with enjoyment to avoid burnout.
We're going to keep our wrist as our barometer for economy of motion – no up‑and‑down, no in‑and‑out, just keep it right there.
Anything we want to be able to execute fast we have to be able to do slow, so practice slow, medium and fast while keeping the wrist stable.
Don't tighten up – keep it loose, relaxed and strong, with no extra movements.
When you're playing fast you need to eliminate extraneous motion, like a drummer on the ride cymbal – focus on economy of motion.
Beautiful job, your playing was awesome – I loved hearing you play again.
What does this piece mean to you? Why did you pick it? Think about the drama, the story, the movie‑scene it could be – that will help you shape the music.

When you reach the tense or unusual measures, lean into them and get a little louder. Think about where the phrases rise and fall, where you can crescendo or decrescendo, and how those phrases build larger climaxes.
Try ‘blocking’ the arpeggios – play the first two beats of a measure as a single unit instead of separate notes. This helps you hear the music in phrases rather than as isolated notes.
Make sure you’re sitting tall, a little forward over the keys, with your wrists level and flexible. A proper 90‑degree seat height will keep your wrists from dropping and your shoulders from tensing.
I challenge you to play this piece with your eyes closed – it will force you to trust your ear and break the habit of watching your fingers.
Beautiful performance – your voicing was amazing, the melody sang above the accompaniment.
Make sure every note is played on the edge of the keys with nice, rounded fingertips. Pay special attention to the thumb on inner voices – keep it light and close to the edge.
Practice the piece slowly at half tempo with a metronome, focusing on keeping every finger on the edge of the key and keeping the thumb quiet. Once the sound is even, gradually speed up.
Don’t rely on the pedal to create legato. If you lift the pedal while your fingers are not truly connected, you lose real legato. Work on finger legato first, then add pedal for color.
Take the pedal away and figure out the fingering so you can make the notes truly legato. When you’re comfortable, add the pedal back in for extra color, but never use it as a crutch.
The first note of a slur is longer and stronger and the second note of the slur is lighter and shorter – that's absolutely fine.
If we do that exaggerated down‑up movement on short Mozart phrases we distort the phrase and it sounds completely wrong.
We need to use a range of dynamics – not the extremes of triple‑piano or triple‑forte, but a full expressive range.
I prefer to play the subject legato, but you could mix legato and staccato, or start legato and become progressively less legato as you go up.
Let me demonstrate playing legato with the same pair of fingers so there’s an almost imperceptible gap between each pair of notes – that’s what we want.
You don’t have to use the same pair of fingers; you could use a more conventional fingering and phrase it with your wrist.
The absence of composer’s markings does not stop us using these touches; it gives us freedom to use anything that conforms to good taste.
I’ll play it wrongly on purpose to show how exaggerated phrasing makes the music sound hiccupy – this is just for illustration.
I hope from this demonstration you’ll feel empowered to use all of the touches we’ve looked at, not just where the composer marked them – playing in a flat way would be boring and unmusical.
In some places it's too loud, especially in the trills in bar 1, bar 7, and bar 21 – it was a bit too loud.
Try to be quite even between your two partners.
You blew the pedal at the end and you are using too much pedal – calm it down.
The left hand and right hand were not together.
You don't kill those ornaments; you're treating it like a rock piece and going crazy with it.
Baroque is a dance style, not a ‘killing’ style – think about the appropriate style for this music.
Do slow practice with separate hands and listen to the sound; you are going straight down too much, that's why the accent comes up.
The timing was very good.
You're exaggerating – you don't need to go that far, just follow the lines.

Cho plays all four in the old‑fashioned way like a daredevil virtuoso super fast floating time and Dynamics without fear of making mistakes and being happy – this description is vague and uses the wrong terminology.

You talk about the Scharos as a clump; it would really make sense to pick one or two passages and give a few specific examples of what you’re referring to rather than saying they’re all played fast and wonderful.
The only thing you say about the second piano concerto is that it’s ‘not bad’ – that tells us absolutely nothing and needs a more detailed musical description.
We need to start by talking about how to rhythmically organize the tempo so you can perform with the orchestra, not just the piano.
You have liberty to take time when the orchestra isn’t involved, but you must be mindful of places where many musicians are playing and you can’t accommodate every jump in tempo.
You have total freedom to express yourself with dynamics, accents, tone colour, vibrato, diminuendos and crescendos – just not with time.
The iritenuto passage must be absolutely straight and not as fast as you played; no crazy tempo changes – if you stretch a chord you must compensate on the next one, the beats have to stay organized.
It would help you to practice with a piano or a recording, look at the score and strategize where you can take time and where you cannot.
Make sure the opening is both rhythmically organized and expressive; it was expressive but not rhythmically solid. Play it more straight and then we’ll hear how the expression works.
One of the fingering principles is that long fingers like to go on black keys and short fingers like to go on white keys.
We want to keep the arm quite light; start with a parachute touch so the fingers can depress the key without extra weight.
Every thumb rotates toward your body as you play the scale.
When you go above one octave, let the left elbow guide the hand upward and the right elbow guide the hand downward.
You can change articulations and dynamics – for example start loud and get softer, or start soft and get louder.
Treat the piano like a canvas; paint the keys with sound to create a line up and down.
Play the scale with the same rhythm throughout; keep the tempo steady so the next notes are predictable.
Always start with a very full‑toned sound at the beginning of the scale.
Use a lot of energy in the fingertip and a lot of motion in the hand so the fingers know where they’re going.
Scales can be beautiful; even a simple C‑major scale can make your mother weep.
Try not to get overwhelmed by the volume of concepts – pick one or two elements to start with and work them gradually into your playing, one hand at a time.
Do the left‑hand alone first, slow, and you can vary the three‑and‑four rhythm or even leave a note out.
In the Oompa sequence keep a steady quarter‑note rhythm between the bottom note and the rest of the chord to bring out a counter‑melody in the lowest note.
Comping in the cracks – lead with the right‑hand improv line and only place the left‑hand chord where the right‑hand line breathes; it’s very syncopated. Let’s try it again.
When you play four‑to‑the‑bar, make sure you don’t play it too loud because it can become overly intense.
Try changing the chord in the middle of the measure – two beats on one voicing, two beats on another – to add movement and variety.
Remember this is an advanced clinic; the best way is to take one or two elements at a time, practice one‑handed alone, then slowly incorporate improvisation.
When improvising in the left hand, generate the motion from your elbow or shoulder rather than just the fingers; it will feel more solid and improve technique immediately.
Generate tone from your legs and feet; thinking of the sound as coming from the ground opens up the resonance.
Combine an on‑beat and an off‑beat comping rhythm – for example one‑two‑one – to create a stop‑time feel.
Double arpeggios: play an arpeggio in each hand a tenth apart; this creates an implied rhythm and a full texture.
Faux stride: keep two levels in the left hand – an octave held as long as you like, then move up or down – the important thing is the two‑level structure.
Rodeo pattern: repeat root‑up‑fifth‑down‑fifth in a direct rhythm; the primary counter‑melody sits in the middle.
Do it a little faster.
That was wrong – just don't put any other notes in it.
Oh, that's wrong – just play the right notes.
You gotta practice that, then you can do it as a blues.
You can also do it backwards.
You can also play it two octaves again as a C9 passing chord.
We’re going to use this one note in the left hand and the other notes in the right hand.
Let's try it like that.
You can actually use this – let's hold the seventh in your left hand.
That was good.
I recommend the Moskovsky School of Double Notes (Op. 64) as the essential resource – it gives fingerings, exercises and studies for double notes.
Keep the wrist aligned with the fingers that are playing; notice how a locked wrist is tiring – let me show you how to align the wrist and arm behind the pair of fingers.
When practicing double‑third scales aim for legato by joining at least one note of each pair and find a fingering that lets you lift the thumb while the other finger stays connected.
To let the fingers cross each other you need a slightly pointed hand, pointing in the direction of travel; a conventional hand position won’t allow the adjustment comfortably.
Think of the double notes as separate voices: play the lower voice softer and the upper voice stronger, then combine the hands.
Use a tapping technique – repeat the lower‑voice note twice while preserving the desired dynamic level, then reverse the idea for a longer, more sustained sound.
Practice broken chords (broken thirds) and keep the fingering you will use in the full double‑note passage; this helps the fingers stay independent.
When working on double sixths, incorporate larger arm movements and practice the passage using a sextuplet subdivision; notice how mobile the wrist stays.
Listen carefully to the quality of sound – the top voice should generally be stronger than the lower voice, giving a clear, beautiful tonal balance.

There’s a much better exercise for practicing your major and minor scales – you’ll learn the notes, the chords that come from the scale, and you’ll learn how to swing your right hand, which is really important in jazz, using the charlon rhythm.
Focus on one key each month, practice 5‑10 minutes a day and aim for a goal tempo of 80 BPM. Once you hit that tempo, move to the next key the following month.
If you can’t play your major and minor chords, you won’t be able to play the jazzier sounding chords that come from them.
For intermediate players, practice each of the seven modes in a key for one month, 5‑10 minutes per day, and work toward a goal tempo of 100 BPM before moving to the next key.
Advanced players should practice the altered scales (whole‑tone, dominant‑diminished, fully altered) for 5‑10 minutes a day, aiming for a goal tempo of 110 BPM before shifting to a new key.
I encourage you to watch all three level videos so you can decide for yourself which level is most appropriate for you.
By the end of the year, if you follow this plan you will have covered all 12 major and minor scales.
Back up a little and sing out toward the room instead of down at the camera so your neck isn’t in a weird position.
You’re moving between the high and low registers of this rangy song really well.
At the start of words like *vago* you’re saying a “z” sound; it should be an “s” sound in Italian.
When you sing *con languido* you’re adding a shadow vowel after the n; make the tongue come up for the n and flow directly into the l.
The Italian “‑n‑” can close the pharyngeal space and feel like a pop; try to hum through it so it doesn’t interrupt the phrase.
In Italian, an n before b or p naturally turns into an m (e.g., *John Paulo* becomes *John Paulo* with an m‑like sound).
When an n is followed by k or a hard g, it becomes an ng sound (e.g., *swan kantar* → *swang kantar*).
If I had too heavy a thumb, it sounds very heavy and clumpy – keep the thumb light.
Practice the thumb line a touch faster than the tempo to encourage lightness.
For wrist octaves keep the wrist slightly higher and flutter from the wrist.
When playing wrist octaves let the arm stay very mobile, taking several wrist strokes in one arm movement to stay loose and relaxed.
Virtuoso octaves need a firm hand and wrist, then bounce from one octave to the next – lift a little from the key surface and bounce.
If the fourth finger pulls the hand out of its natural position, avoid it; use the fifth finger instead – it’s fine to use all‑fifth‑finger fingering when needed.
Hold an octave while keeping the wrist very supple, using easy down‑up wrist movements.
Hang on to the fifth finger while the thumb moves chromatically; feel mobility in the thumb tip and a firm but relaxed fifth finger.
Practice the fifth finger alone, then combine it with a rebounding thumb – the bounce comes from the forearm.
Use a tiny forearm rotation; exaggerate it slightly in practice to gain a great sense of freedom in the arm.
When repeating the fifth finger, keep the rotary movement and add a tenuto quality to build firmness.
Avoid a low wrist that creates tension; keep the thumb light, the fifth finger firm, and never let the wrist drop below a comfortable level.
Take note of what I'm doing in the left hand – the bouncing back and forth between a root note and a chord.
One of the key differences between the left hand of ragtime and the left hand of stride is that stride started to cover greater distances on the piano.

If you're not practiced with it it's really hard to do because you have to imagine jumping onto these different shapes, speed up, and just stab at it until you really work at it and eventually you'll get it down.
The inclusion of the second sound to create a slightly detuned effect is very similar to the sound of a honky‑tonk piano.
People often work on repeated notes too hard; you don’t have to lift the fingers off the key surface for each repeated note – stay close to the keyboard and avoid a stiff, lifted staccato.
Don’t think of the passage as counting ‘one‑two‑three’; feel the flow as it moves from here to here to here.
Treat the repeated‑note triplet as a single motion: lift your hand slightly and let the notes emerge together rather than re‑articulating each one.
For the sharp, accented attacks you need lively fingers, then immediately ease off on longer notes – release the tension so you don’t get stuck.
Make sure you choreograph the left hand so it isn’t tied up with the right; keep the hands independent.
When you reach the loud, banging sections, make sure every note spits out clearly and the counter‑rhythms are audible.
Even if nobody hears every detail, you’ll feel good about yourself when you get it right.
Rachmaninoff’s texture has two accompaniment layers beneath the melody; if those layers aren’t balanced the theme sounds weak, so listen to the inner voices as well as the tune.
If your nine sharps aren’t perfectly in tune, the effect is far worse than a small rhythmic slip at the next bar.
I use a ‘semi‑cheat’: play the rapid repeated notes with the left hand in a 2‑1 pattern; it reduces stress and lets the hand stay relaxed. Try it if you find the standard fingering too hard.
You have to keep doing ear‑training your whole life – you must be able to recognize intervals and chord qualities by ear.
The basic formula of a major chord is 1‑3‑5 (root, major third, perfect fifth).
An augmented chord is 1‑3‑♯5 – the only difference from a major chord is a raised fifth.
A minor chord is 1‑♭3‑5 – you flatten the third of the major triad.
A diminished chord is 1‑♭3‑♭5 – you flatten both the third and the fifth of the major triad.
To memorize these formulas, write them down and repeat them over and over.
A sus2 chord uses the formula 1‑2‑5 (root, second, fifth).
A sus4 chord uses the formula 1‑4‑5 (root, fourth, fifth).
The Lydian triad is built as 1‑♯4‑5 (root, raised fourth, fifth).
The Phrygian triad is built as 1‑♭2‑5 (root, lowered second, fifth).
Practice hearing each triad repeatedly; then do test rounds that track your score so you can gauge your listening accuracy.
When you hear the first chord of a song you can predict the rest of the progression if you know the key – that’s why strong ear‑training lets you figure out songs quickly.
Each degree of the major scale has a related chord (I, ii, iii, IV, V, vi, vii°); knowing these relationships helps you analyse any piece.
Treat suspended chords as ‘constant structures’ – for every scale degree you can raise or lower the third to create sus2 or sus4 variations.
Congratulations, you now know all the white keys on a piano.
One C is an octave higher than the other; the lower C has half the frequency of the higher C.
I’ll touch the black keys just a little bit – this is C♯, D♯, F♯, G♯ and A♯ (also called flats).
The distance between D and E is a whole step, while the distance between E and F is a half step.
The C‑major scale pattern is: whole, whole, half, whole, whole, whole, half.
Assign a number to each note: C = 1, D = 2, E = 3, etc.
If we play the 1‑3‑5 notes together we have a C‑major chord.
Move the same hand shape to the A note; the 1‑3‑5 there gives an A‑minor chord.
Each degree of the C‑major scale has a mode name – I (Ionian), II (Dorian), III (Phrygian), etc.
A chord progression is just a sequence of these numbered chords; the quality (major/minor) depends on the interval between the root and the third.
Add the seventh degree (7) to the 1‑3‑5 pattern and you get a C‑major‑7 chord.
The 1 and 5 notes are ‘best friends’ on the circle of fifths; notes close on the circle sound more related.
Starting from G, the same whole‑step/half‑step pattern gives the G‑major scale.
Playing the same chord shape starting on a different note creates inversions – first inversion, second inversion, etc.
Changing the 1‑3‑5 formula to 1‑2‑5 makes a suspended‑second chord; 1‑4‑5 makes a suspended‑fourth.
The pentatonic scale uses five notes instead of seven and sounds good no matter how you play it.
You can think of the black keys as the pentatonic scale – you can’t really play ‘bad’ with it.

I normally suggest that students play the same number of active fingers when they learn scales and arpeggios together; usually two for intermediate level and four for advanced level. I avoid three‑finger groupings because the odd number makes the split awkward.

You could play the passage faster if you curve your fingers more. Right now your second and fourth fingers need to lift higher so that all three knuckles are visible – a well‑curved finger gives better control.
Practice the hands separately; this will help you hit each note more precisely.
In the second section you slowed down to about 72 bpm, which is much slower than the indicated ‘poco lento’. Try not to reduce the tempo that much; the change should be only a little slower.
You presented the cheerful opening melody very well and kept a solid tempo around 92 bpm.
When you read the score, take time to look at it carefully and notice all the detailed changes – for example the D♯‑to‑E transition that the composer wrote to make a smooth modulation.
The composer wrote D♯ in the first part so that it resolves nicely to E; that’s why the transition sounds smoother there than in the later phrase.
In the third section the left hand plays octave 16th‑notes; you need to be ready to launch them at tempo immediately – this is the most challenging part of the piece.
You’re using only the first and fifth fingers for the rapid passages. Try using the fourth finger on the black keys and the fifth finger on the white keys; this makes the runs easier and faster.
Overall you’re an early‑advanced adult amateur pianist. I appreciate your dedication and passion and look forward to seeing you perform in a concert hall one day.
You have very good fingering for your scale.
Maintain consistency and try not to stop in the middle; practice one octave at a time, repeat several times, then add another octave, and later the descending pattern.
The correct fingering for the right hand is 4‑1‑2‑4‑1‑2‑4 and for the left hand 2‑1‑4‑2‑1‑4‑2; double‑check your fingerings and use the fourth finger for the C note instead of the third.
I like the dynamic contrast you made in the middle of the piece.
Your tempo is inconsistent—you slow down at measures 5‑7 and 13; keep a steady tempo unless a ritardando is indicated, and use a metronome to practice.
I like the dynamic contrast you created in this piece.
Play more playfully and lightly, which requires a faster tempo; try increasing the tempo gradually from 70 BPM to 80, then 90, then 100.
I’m going to show you how to manage trills and how to feel comfortable with them because a lot of people feel awkward, tight and stiff.
I call trills ‘chameleons’ – they should fit the surrounding music, bright‑fast for a lively piece, slower and softer for a lyrical section.
Notice how I shape my trills so that they are melodic rather than just fast, rhythmic ornaments.
A trill can be energetic, lyrical, or decorative – choose its character to match the mood of the piece.
Here are two ways to play the same trill – first measured exactly, second free and flowing. I prefer the free version for this lyrical passage.
Give the trill a shape: linger a little on the first note, accelerate in the middle, then slow down again at the end.
Some trills, like cadential ones, should be as strong and loud as possible and as fast as you can make them.
Notice my low wrist and rotary movement – I’m not articulating with the fingers themselves, they just sit on the keys while the arm does the work.
When a trill is paired with the other hand, practice it first measured (1:1, then 2:1, then 3:1, etc.) so the hands stay coordinated.
Practice three measured variations of a trill – straight, with a rhythmic twist, and ending with a triplet – each deliberately and slowly.
Try different fingerings: 3‑2, 3‑1, 4‑2, or the 1‑3‑2‑3 pattern with an undulating arm movement to avoid fatigue.
Because of the double‑escapement mechanism you only need to lift the key part‑way; combine this with a gentle arm undulation for a free‑feeling trill.
Make your trills even – no bumps or sudden accelerations – unless you deliberately choose an unmeasured, free style.
Use Mozart’s trill exercise with a metronome, starting slowly and gradually increasing speed to build control.
Try to keep your hand relaxed and avoid tension; change your mood quickly between sections.
The overall intensity should be a little stronger – play with more weight.
You need to put more force to move forward; it shouldn’t be an easy‑going, floating feeling.
At the end of the right‑hand phrase you should leave a small gap; you’re rushing a little now.
There is a dynamic contrast here – you can’t keep the same volume, you have to shape the phrase emotionally.
Your touch is a bit too lyrical; try a shorter, clearer pedal to avoid a dry, narrow sound.
Remember you’re the boss of the tempo – stay on time and don’t let the music drift.
Exaggerate the character a little more and add a heavier touch; you can also use a bit more pedal for contrast.
The structure is getting lost; use less pedal so the harmonic changes are clearer.
Start the next section very clearly, almost without pedal, then add pedal and dynamics as the phrase develops.
Don’t rush the simple passages and don’t slow down too much; change the pedal for every harmony, not just the F.
What you're doing is trying to do two things at once with your right hand, and that's very hard.
Try to give your right hand as much free reign as possible to play the melody.
Practice the left‑hand accompaniment bits separately so you can keep the melody clear.
One of the quickest ways to kill your melody is to press the keys straight down; it will never sound lyrical.
Think of the piano like a string instrument – the fingers are the bow, so try to move the hands in and out like a bow.
Tap your knee one‑two while you play to feel the duple against the triplet rhythm.
You’re letting the triplet feel dominate the line; practice the passage with each hand separately to keep the rhythm clean.
It’s important to ‘vibrate’ in the soft dynamic; you did a nice soft sound there – keep that lyrical quality.
That was a wonderful performance – you just got up and nailed it, your face is full of story and you really brought the song to life.
You’re not doing anything wrong with the German, but there are a couple of things that could be easier – let’s work on the vowel shape.
I love how the song has that huge arc – you navigated the long build cleanly, keep that feeling alive.
You started on the safety‑blanket note – was that intentional? What does that represent for the character as she descends into madness?
When she’s thinking about him during the quiet build, she’s constantly imagining his features – that’s why she sounds unbearably sad.
Be careful about closing your eyes completely or looking at the floor; use the piano to ground you but don’t hang on it. Keep your gaze up so the audience can see you.
To get the German ‘a’ vowel, place the roof of your mouth, put the tongue in the NG position, feel the teeth, then let go a little – that’s how you shape the sound.
You’re not quite there yet – you need to feel the teeth when you release, otherwise the vowel sounds off. Try saying ‘head’ while keeping that tooth contact.
Your high A’s at the end have a really clean vowel – that’s impressive! Maybe give yourself a little more space on those notes, but don’t make them too difficult.
Play the C‑diminished‑7 chord, then move the C up a half‑step and hit it again – that’s the movement you need to hear.
When you lower the note in the diminished‑7 chord you get a dominant‑7 (B7 is the dominant of E). You should be able to substitute that for the dominant B.
If you raise one note of a diminished‑7 chord you get a minor‑7 flat‑5 (or a minor‑6). Raise any note and you’ll get the same type of chord.
Just play the pattern E‑C‑E‑G‑A and then move the E and G up a half‑step – that’s how you get the next sound.
We must learn to play horizontally – think in terms of movements, not just vertical chord stacks. Move from one note to the next by raising a note a half‑step, then drop it back.
You have to run B7 into D7, B7 into F7, etc. Why would you run B7 into A7? Think about what each substitution does for the harmony.
Horn players don’t know how to improvise; you can’t learn improvisation just from knowing chords on the piano.
You should be able to take a phrase, start on a note and play a rhythm in B‑flat, even run Giant Steps the way a cat should.
The only way to play Giant Steps is to play one beat on B and immediately go to D.
You have to get right quick and go; you must change notes immediately, not think about chords first.
If I go from E♭ I would never play it that way; I would voice the F7 to the third of D differently.
I want to hear some triplets in there; I don’t want just straight eighth‑notes.
Let me show you how Will Davis used to end a song – he would just say, ‘And that’s the same thing.’
You should be able to start on a note and instantly move to the next chord tone, like going from G to B♭ in one beat.
Well done – that was a very nice performance of this critical piece.
It’s a very difficult work; there are a lot of technical and musical problems to sort out.
A polonaise has a strong rhythmic emphasis; you need to keep the ‘zip’ in the rhythm and let the fantasy sections breathe – develop your own personality in the music.
Let’s try the opening again – I don’t feel the connection through the whole phrase. Keep your wrist flexible so the thumb can move to the next note and play the opening with a strong pulse.
On the third version, start a little softer and then build the dynamics up gradually.
Try to imagine words or a story that go with the music – it can help you shape the phrases.
Don’t slow down too much; keep the tempo steady so the first melodic statement remains clear.
Stay a little longer before you launch into the arpeggios – give the phrase a bit more breathing space.
Take a brief pause at the top to make the entry special; notice there’s no diminuendo on the third passage and the G‑natural adds a striking colour change.
Remember the fermata on these measures – hold the note as indicated.
Try to eliminate any extra notes that aren’t written in the score; listen carefully to the pure line.
The metronome is one of the greatest tools you can use – make sure you practice with it and have the right fingering.
You need the right technique – keep your fingers in the correct position and use proper fingering.
Make sure your playing is clean so people can understand what you’re doing.
Speed depends on the genre – classical music usually requires more speed, but it’s good to build up your speed anyway so you’re ready for any style.
Scales gave me the ability to understand key centers, develop melody, and build chords – they’re the foundation for those musical concepts.
When you start hearing a run of notes, you can recognize it as a scale; that helps you pick out bass notes and construct the harmony.
Practice makes better – repeat the same material over and over and you’ll get faster and cleaner each day.
Playing for an audience of one in your private time shapes how you sound publicly – keep that personal connection with the music.
It's all about independence of the fingers.
You do not want to shake around your elbow or get really tense; you want to stay relaxed.
Lift up only your third finger and pinky while holding the other fingers down.
I want to put this in a rhythm, so I’ll play the chord and then each combination in a steady one‑two pattern.
Mirrored position is a really great way to practice; play the same fingers in the opposite hand.
We start at a beginner level and slowly graduate until you feel comfortable moving each finger separately.
The progression moves from major to minor to diminished to dominant, showing how each finger moves a half step.
Very fantastic job with the transcription and everything like that.
You’ve written the violin part in a very comfortable range… violinists can generally play whatever you write right in the middle of the staff, so it’s quite playable.
The tempo may be a little bit wild, but I thought that was lovely and very interesting.
Giving the viola the melody higher up in the treble clef is an interesting choice – the viola sounds fantastic up there even though we usually keep its melody in a deeper range.
I really like this arrangement – it evokes the stressful feeling of Day 3 perfectly. Great job!
In the 12/8 measure you’ve hidden beat three. Split the second quarter‑note into two eighth‑notes tied together so that beat three is visible; it will be easier to sight‑read.
For the next measure, split the dotted‑quarter note into an eighth‑note tied to a quarter‑note (or just make it an eighth‑note and add a rest) so the rhythm is clear and the notation cleaner.
Silence is very effective, but the half‑rest at the end of measure 2 feels ambiguous. Add something there so the opening statement of the piece isn’t an unclear long silence.
The secret to better harmony isn’t to learn more chords – you need to think about how the voices move, not just stack blocks of chords.
Stop thinking about chords and start thinking about voice‑leading; that shift will change how you write because voice‑leading lets you shape your harmony in new ways.

Listen to the bottom two voices – they slip down just one note, then the top voice slips down a half step, then the bottom voice slips down another half step. One voice at a time the music slips lower and lower.
When you want more emotional urgency, accelerate the harmonic rhythm – go from three chords in a bar to four chords in a bar.
At the climax make the chord the loudest dynamic yet and put it in the lowest register of the piece.
Use chromatic side‑slipping: one voice moves a half step, then another voice moves a half step, and the music lands back in familiar territory smoothly.
You can even jump a full tritone – the most distant interval – and make it sound sublime if you guide the voices with good voice‑leading.
Build a five‑note chord full of colour and explore all the subtle ways each voice moves; that’s where your own voice comes through.
